policy_statement: Research landfill and anaerobic digestion emissions to evaluate monitoring techniques, quantify fugitive emissions, and evaluate best gas uses during electrification transition; research regulatory frameworks/best practices to minimize methane and co-pollutant emissions from AD/RNG.
mechanism_description: It calls for research to improve monitoring and estimation of landfill and anaerobic digestion/RNG methane emissions and to identify best-practice and potential regulatory approaches that reduce fugitive methane and co-pollutant releases.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management operations → ↓ fugitive CH₄ leakage (via monitoring + controls) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: How landfills and anaerobic digestion/RNG systems are monitored, managed, and regulated (e.g., better detection, tighter operating standards, improved capture/containment). (2) Mediating process: Improved quantification and detection of leaks enables operational fixes and/or set controls (e.g., sealing leaks, optimizing gas collection, preventing venting), reducing unintended methane release during waste decomposition and gas handling. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane emissions (and often co-pollutants/VOCs), producing direct GHG mitigation with strong near-term benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing fugitive CH₄ emissions from landfill and anaerobic digestion/RNG systems, thereby lowering near-term greenhouse gas forcing.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: interventions in waste-sector methane measurement and operational/regulatory practices primarily aim to cut fugitive methane, directly reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: although better monitoring is an enabling action, its intended and largest climate outcome is emissions reduction (Mitigation), not adaptation, resource efficiency, or nature-based carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a program were purely “inventory improvement” with no expected operational or regulatory follow-through, climate impact could be indirect; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly includes best practices/regulatory approaches to reduce emissions, keeping Mitigation dominant. Co-pollutant reduction is ancillary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy under this mechanism (monitoring/estimation + best practices/regulation for landfill/AD/RNG methane) has the main effect of lowering fugitive methane emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: whether a specific policy is chiefly R&D, regulatory, or operational (and the magnitude of reductions) depends on policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If framed solely as research/MRV with no linkage to operational change or enforceable standards, benefits may be enabling rather than direct; if the best-practice/regulatory component is present (as described), Mitigation remains the correct primary category.
key_indicators: “DEC should research”; “evaluate monitoring techniques”; “quantify fugitive GHG emissions”; “methods to improve estimates of methane emissions”; “strategies to reduce or eliminate these emissions”; “consideration of a regulatory framework”; “best practices to minimize methane and co-pollutant emissions”
co_benefits: Air quality improvements via reduced “co-pollutant emissions” (e.g., VOCs/odors), and potential energy system optimization via “most appropriate uses for the gas during the transition to statewide electrification.”
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as “information-disclosure” if it required public reporting/MRV, but the text emphasizes research/evaluation (agenda-setting) rather than mandated disclosure or standards; the “regulatory framework” is only prospective.
